prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white female, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. For this one-income household with two young children, I’d target **9 months of essential expenses** in emergency savings. The mortgage-free home lowers the required dollars, but the unemployed spouse, single-income dependence, childcare needs, and home-repair exposure justify more than the standard 3–6 months.

| Monthly essential expenses | Minimum: 6 months | Recommended: 9 months | Conservative: 12 months |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | $18,000 | **$27,000** | $36,000 |
| $4,000 | $24,000 | **$36,000** | $48,000 |
| $5,000 | $30,000 | **$45,000** | $60,000 |

**Practical target:** likely **$30,000–$45,000**, depending on actual essential spending. Race and age do not materially determine the target; income stability, necessary expenses, insurance, and available support do.

Calculate the exact target as:

> **9 × monthly essentials**, including property taxes, homeowners insurance, utilities, groceries, transportation, healthcare, childcare, minimum debt payments, and necessary home maintenance.

Keep this fund in an insured high-yield savings or money-market deposit account—not stocks. Because the home is owned outright, maintain a **separate home-repair reserve** if major systems such as the roof or HVAC are aging; otherwise, one large repair could consume the family’s unemployment cushion.

This is educational guidance; a qualified financial planner can tailor it to employment benefits, insurance coverage, and actual expenses.
